title: Inkle woven belt
subtitle: A warp-faced band in two colours on an inkle loom
excerpt: An inkle loom produces narrow warp-faced bands by alternately raising and lowering groups of warp threads. The weft is invisible: the pattern is made entirely from the arrangement of coloured warp threads. This project makes a belt or bag strap approximately 180 cm long and 3 cm wide.
sourceNotes: Inkle loom weaving is a well-documented craft. The warp-faced band technique is described in inkle weaving texts and in the Ashford and Schacht inkle loom documentation. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A frame loom with a series of pegs around which warp is wound in a continuous loop. Some warp threads pass through heddle loops; the rest pass free above them. Toggling gives two sheds.
A weave structure in which the weft is beaten so firmly that it is completely hidden by the warp threads. All visible colour and pattern comes from the arrangement of warp colours.
On an inkle loom, a small loop of string attached to a fixed peg that encircles alternate warp threads. When the free threads are pressed down, the heddle threads rise to create a shed.
The order in which warp threads of different colours are threaded onto the inkle loom. The colour sequence determines the stripe or check pattern that appears on the finished band.

content.text: bands in which all the pattern comes from the
of the warp. This belt uses two alternating colours to produce a simple stripe. For a check pattern, pair colours so that each
thread and the adjacent free thread are different colours.

items.name: Cotton or linen yarn, DK weight, colour A (main)
Cotton or linen yarn, DK weight, colour B (contrast)
Stick shuttle or small flat stick for the weft

content.text: Tie colour A to the first peg. Wind each warp thread in a continuous loop around the pegs following the loom's warping diagram, alternating: one thread through a heddle loop (heddle thread), next thread over the heddle peg but not through a loop (free thread). For a two-colour stripe, wind colour A for the heddle threads and colour B for the free threads, alternating throughout. A 3 cm wide belt at 10 ends per 2.5 cm requires approximately 12-14 warp ends total.
Step 2: Open the shed and begin weaving

Press the free threads down: the heddle threads rise to open the first shed. Pass the shuttle through this shed from right to left, leaving a 5 cm tail. Beat the weft firmly down toward the woven portion. The weft should not be visible at all from the front once beaten.

Lift the free threads up (the heddle threads drop) to open the second shed. Pass the shuttle back through from left to right. Beat. Continue alternating, one pick per shed, across the full length of the belt. Advance the warp around the loom periodically as the woven length builds up behind the cloth beam.

When the belt reaches 180 cm (or the desired length), cut from the loom leaving 6 cm warp ends at each end. Fold the warp ends back along the belt underside and sew them down flat with a blunt darning needle and a length of weft colour, or knot them in groups of two and trim.

items.symptom: Weft is visible on the belt surface
items.cause: Not enough warp threads packed closely enough, or the weft was not beaten firmly into the fell.
items.fix: Beat each pick very firmly. For future warps, increase the warp density (more ends per 2.5 cm) or use a heavier warp yarn.

items.symptom: Belt pulls to one side and is not straight
items.cause: Uneven warp tension across the width, or the shuttle was pulled at an angle when passing through the shed.
items.fix: Check that all warp ends feel equally taut across the full width. Pass the shuttle at 90 degrees to the warp, not at an angle.
